pattern: Expanding a subarray's cost shows it is linear in the cost-prefix-sum of everything before its end, so partitioning becomes a layered dynamic program where each layer (subarray count so far) is solved with a monotonic lower-envelope of lines instead of scanning every earlier split point.
hints: Let dp[i][j] be the minimum total cost to split the first i elements into exactly j subarrays. A transition from split point t to i pays (globalPrefixNums[i] + k*j) times (prefixCost[i] - prefixCost[t]) for the j-th piece.

Expand that product: for a fixed j, as i grows the multiplier (globalPrefixNums[i] + k*j) only grows, and each candidate predecessor t defines a line in that multiplier with slope -prefixCost[t] and intercept dp[t][j-1] -- you want the minimum line at each query point.

Since the candidate lines' slopes are added in decreasing order and the query points only increase, maintain a monotonic deque of lines (a convex hull trick) per subarray-count layer instead of testing every t, and take the best total cost over every possible final subarray count.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, best split is two subarrays
statement example, best split is three subarrays
a single element, so only one subarray is possible
the maximum allowed order penalty k with a short array
every element identical, so the split choice is driven entirely by the order penalty and cost weights
cost is concentrated on one element, rewarding a split that isolates it into its own late, cheap-multiplier subarray
a slightly longer array with mixed values and a moderate order penalty

stress.description: random arrays up to the maximum length with random cost weights and order penalty
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(1000, size))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    cost = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 1000)
    return [nums, cost, k]
